subsystem: ImageMagick raster-image decoder: ReadARTImage(), the coder that parses the ART (PFS/1st-Publisher '.art') bi-level image format into a MagickCore Image. It reads a tiny fixed header (a discarded word, columns, a discarded word, rows) from the image blob, then decodes one packed 1-bit gray scanline per row into pixel packets via the QuantumInfo import path.
provenance: Identified by the MagickCore coder idiom and API surface: the static Read<Format>Image(const ImageInfo*, ExceptionInfo*) signature, MagickCoreSignature assertions, AcquireImage/OpenBlob/CloseBlob/DestroyImageList blob lifecycle, ReadBlobLSBShort/ReadBlobStream, the QuantumInfo pipeline (AcquireQuantumInfo, GetQuantumExtent, GetQuantumPixels, ImportQuantumPixels, DestroyQuantumInfo), and the ThrowReaderException/ThrowFileException macros. This is coders/art.c from ImageMagick. The in-band tag on line 1, '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2044_fixed */', additionally marks it as a curated (fixed) sample, but the review is performed on the code's own merits.
trust_boundary: The entire input is an attacker-supplied image file. OpenBlob (line 34) opens a caller-named file/stream in binary read mode; every subsequent ReadBlobLSBShort/ReadBlobStream pulls bytes straight from that untrusted blob. image->columns and image->rows (lines 43,45) are 16-bit little-endian values taken verbatim from the file and are fully hostile; they drive the per-row buffer sizing and the loop trip count. The pixel bytes read each iteration (line 72) are hostile and are fed into ImportQuantumPixels. image_info and exception are trusted engine-internal structures (guarded by MagickCoreSignature asserts).

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the entry point of the ART-format image decoder inside ImageMagick's coder framework. It takes a const ImageInfo* (engine configuration and the target filename/blob descriptor) and an ExceptionInfo* (out-parameter for error reporting), returning a newly built Image* or NULL on failure. Being 'static' it is registered as the format's read callback; MagickCore invokes it whenever a blob is sniffed/typed as ART. Everything it reads originates from the untrusted image file.

Local state. 'pixels' points at the raw bytes returned by ReadBlobStream for the current scanline. 'image' is the object under construction. 'quantum_info' is the heap resource that will describe how packed sample bytes map to pixel quanta; it is the resource whose lifetime dominates this review. 'status' captures MagickBooleanType results. 'length' (size_t) is the byte count of one packed scanline. 'count' (ssize_t) receives the number of bytes actually read, and 'y' is the row index (signed so it can be compared against the (ssize_t)-cast row count).

Precondition checks on the trusted engine structures: both image_info and exception must be non-NULL and carry the correct MagickCoreSignature, catching internal API misuse. Optional trace logging echoes the filename when debug is enabled. These asserts validate the caller side of the boundary, not the untrusted file contents.

AcquireImage allocates the Image object (first tracked resource). OpenBlob attaches the untrusted input stream in binary read mode. If the blob cannot be opened, the image list is destroyed (freeing the just-acquired Image and any attached blob state) and NULL is returned. This is the correct clean-up idiom for the pre-quantum stage: quantum_info has not been allocated yet, so nothing else needs releasing here.

The ART header is parsed. Depth is fixed at 1 bit (bi-level) and endianness is set. The format is a sequence of little-endian 16-bit words: word 0 is discarded, word 1 becomes image->columns (width), word 2 is discarded, word 3 becomes image->rows (height). ReadBlobLSBShort returns an unsigned short (0..65535) and yields 0 on short read/EOF, so columns and rows are attacker-controlled 16-bit magnitudes cast to size_t. No arithmetic is derived from them yet.

A basic sanity gate: a zero width or height is rejected as a corrupt header. ThrowReaderException is a macro that records the exception on 'image', destroys the image list, and returns NULL. This gate also happens to reject the truncated-header case (where the shorts read back as 0). At this point quantum_info is still unallocated, so the macro's implicit image destruction fully cleans up.

Ping mode is a metadata-only path: when the caller only wants dimensions/type, the blob is closed and the image (now carrying columns/rows) is returned without decoding pixels. Again quantum_info has not been allocated, so no quantum clean-up is owed on this exit.

SetImageExtent commits the pixel cache to columns x rows, performing the large multiplication and allocation internally (and enforcing resource limits). On failure the image list is destroyed and NULL is returned. This is the point where the hostile 16-bit dimensions are turned into a real allocation; ImageMagick's own limit checks are the guard against absurd sizes here.

The image is declared grayscale, then AcquireQuantumInfo allocates the QuantumInfo descriptor plus its internal per-row staging pixel buffer (the buffer later returned by GetQuantumPixels). If that allocation fails the reader throws (and because quantum_info is NULL there is nothing extra to release). GetQuantumExtent computes 'length' = the number of packed bytes in one GrayQuantum scanline for the current columns/depth; this same computation is what sizes the GetQuantumPixels buffer, so 'length' and that buffer are meant to stay in lock-step.

The scanline loop runs once per row. QueueAuthenticPixels reserves a 1-row-by-columns region of the authentic pixel cache and returns a writable quantum pointer q; if it fails the loop breaks (falling through to the shared clean-up at line 89). ReadBlobStream then reads exactly 'length' packed bytes from the untrusted blob into the QuantumInfo staging buffer (GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info)) and reports the byte count actually read in 'count'. 'pixels' aliases that staging buffer.

The short-read guard: if fewer (or more) than 'length' bytes were read, the file is truncated/corrupt. Crucially, before throwing, the code releases quantum_info via DestroyQuantumInfo and nulls it. This ordering is the resource-safety point of the whole function: ThrowReaderException destroys the Image but does NOT know about quantum_info, so the quantum resource must be freed manually first, otherwise it would leak on this early return. When the read is complete, ImportQuantumPixels unpacks the 'pixels' bytes into the queued authentic quanta as GrayQuantum samples.

ART scanlines are padded to a 2-byte boundary. The expression (size_t)(-(ssize_t)length) & 0x01 evaluates to 1 when 'length' is odd and 0 when it is even, i.e. it consumes the single pad byte only when the row byte count is odd. That pad byte is read into the same staging buffer and its return/count are ignored. SyncAuthenticPixels flushes the decoded row to the pixel cache (break on failure), and SetImageProgress updates the progress monitor (break if cancelled). Both break paths lead to the shared clean-up below, so quantum_info is still released.

The normal-completion tail. SetQuantumImageType tags the image type. quantum_info is destroyed exactly once here for every path that reached the loop and left it via completion or 'break' (the throw path at line 76 already destroyed it and returned, so there is no double free). EOFBlob is checked to report an unexpected end-of-file; note ThrowFileException here only records the exception and does NOT return, so execution proceeds to CloseBlob and returns the (already-built) first image in the list. This is why the manual DestroyQuantumInfo precedes the EOF check rather than following it.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: quantum_info is a heap resource whose owner is this function alone: neither ThrowReaderException nor ThrowFileException frees it. Once acquired at line 60, EVERY exit that follows must destroy it. This code does: the corrupt-data early return at lines 76-77 destroys it before throwing, and the completion/break paths destroy it at line 89. The fragile invariant (CWE-772) is that any future error branch inserted between line 60 and line 89 that returns/throws without a preceding DestroyQuantumInfo would leak the QuantumInfo and its staging buffer. Because the decoder is reachable from untrusted uploads, a regression here becomes a per-request memory leak and a denial-of-service vector under repeated malformed inputs. The current code upholds the contract; the risk is entirely latent/regression-shaped.

ReadBlobStream writes 'length' attacker-influenced bytes into GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info), and ImportQuantumPixels then interprets those bytes as one row of GrayQuantum samples. The safety invariant the implementation must uphold is that GetQuantumExtent (which produced 'length' at line 63) and the GetQuantumPixels staging buffer (sized inside AcquireQuantumInfo) are computed from the SAME columns/depth so that 'length' never exceeds the destination buffer, and that ImportQuantumPixels consumes no more than 'length' bytes for 'columns' pixels. If those two computations ever diverge (e.g. a depth/colorspace mismatch or a columns value not honored consistently), this becomes an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) into the quantum buffer, or an over-read of 'pixels' during import (CWE-125). In current MagickCore these are matched by construction, so this is a latent contract to confirm rather than a demonstrated overflow.

The four header shorts are read without checking ReadBlobLSBShort's success individually; on a truncated header the function returns 0, which flows into columns/rows. The only guard is the zero-dimension check at line 46, which does catch a fully truncated header but would accept any nonzero 16-bit pair. The upper bound is naturally capped at 65535x65535 by the 16-bit reads, so there is no classic size multiplication overflow here (size_t handles it and SetImageExtent enforces resource limits at line 53). The residual concern is only resource consumption from a legitimately large-but-empty declared canvas, which SetImageExtent's limits are expected to bound.

The pad-byte read ignores both its return value and the resulting 'count'. If the final scanline's pad byte is missing (truncated file), no error is raised at this site; correctness relies on the downstream EOFBlob check at line 90 to surface UnexpectedEndOfFile. This is a benign unchecked-return (CWE-252) rather than a memory-safety issue: the read length is 0 or 1, it targets the already-sized staging buffer, and a short read cannot over-write anything. Worth confirming only that ignoring 'count' here cannot leave a stale 'pixels'/'count' misused later (it is not — 'pixels' is reassigned next iteration before use).

summary: A tightly-scoped ImageMagick ART bi-level decoder whose dominant security contract is the manual lifetime of quantum_info (CWE-772): the code correctly releases it on every exit after allocation, including before the mid-loop corrupt-data throw, so no leak is present in the shown code. Residual risk is low and mostly latent/regression-shaped: the QuantumInfo staging-buffer vs GetQuantumExtent size agreement and the unchecked pad-byte read are contracts to confirm, not demonstrated bugs. This reads as a hardened/fixed reader with no confirmed memory-safety defect.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Callback signature; image_info/exception are trusted engine structures validated by MagickCoreSignature asserts.
Alias into the quantum staging buffer; reassigned before each use, not a standalone buffer.
Manually-managed heap resource that no Throw macro frees; its release on every path is the central CWE-772 contract.
Drives the per-row ReadBlobStream copy size into the quantum buffer; must equal the buffer's allocated extent.
count is the short-read check operand; y is a bounded loop index cast to ssize_t against rows. No buffer contract.
Trusted-caller precondition checks; not attacker-reachable input.
Correct pre-quantum clean-up: DestroyImageList on OpenBlob failure, nothing else allocated yet.
Attacker-controlled 16-bit dimension fields; unchecked per-read success, only a zero-check guard follows.
Correct reject of 0 width/height; quantum_info not yet allocated so no leak on this throw.
Metadata-only path; blob closed and image returned before any quantum allocation.
Allocation with engine resource limits; failure destroys image list, quantum_info not yet allocated.
Acquisition point of the tracked resource; NULL path throws safely but establishes the ownership window.
Row byte count; must match the GetQuantumPixels staging buffer size or the ReadBlobStream copy overflows.
Trip count bounded by 16-bit rows; signed cast is safe for values <= 65535.
Failure breaks to shared clean-up at line 89; quantum_info still released.
Copies untrusted bytes into the quantum buffer; size agreement with the buffer is the OOB-write invariant.
The resource-safety fix: quantum_info must be freed before the early throw or it leaks (CWE-772).
Consumes 'length' bytes for 'columns' quanta; safe as long as the length/buffer contract holds (covered by the length finding).
Reads 0/1 pad byte with return value and count ignored (CWE-252); benign but worth noting.
Both break to the shared clean-up; no resource escapes.
Single normal-path release; ThrowFileException does not return so DestroyQuantumInfo correctly precedes it, avoiding both leak and double free.

findings.title: QuantumInfo lifetime must be manually released on every exit (resource-leak contract, satisfied here)
findings.bug_class: Missing release of memory/resource after effective lifetime (CWE-772) — latent/regression contract
findings.invariant: Once quantum_info is acquired at line 60, every subsequent return/throw path must call DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info) exactly once before leaving, because neither ThrowReaderException nor ThrowFileException frees it.
findings.failure_mode: If any error branch between lines 60 and 89 returns without first destroying quantum_info (the exact bug this sample's fix addresses at lines 76-77, where DestroyQuantumInfo precedes ThrowReaderException on the count!=length corrupt-data path), the QuantumInfo descriptor and its staging buffer leak on each malformed image, giving a remote, unauthenticated memory-exhaustion DoS under repeated hostile uploads. As shown the contract is upheld on all paths (corrupt-data throw at 76, break paths and completion at 89, and there is no double free because the throw path returns), so this is a latent/regression risk rather than a live leak.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm DestroyQuantumInfo runs on every path after line 60 and only once; specifically that no future early-return is added after AcquireQuantumInfo without releasing quantum_info first, and that the line-76 destroy-then-throw ordering is preserved.

findings.title: Per-row copy size (length) must not exceed the quantum staging buffer
findings.bug_class: Potential out-of-bounds write into quantum buffer if extent and buffer size diverge
findings.invariant: GetQuantumExtent(image,quantum_info,GrayQuantum) must return a byte count that is <= the capacity of GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info), and ImportQuantumPixels must not consume more than 'length' bytes for 'image->columns' quanta.
findings.failure_mode: ReadBlobStream writes 'length' untrusted bytes into GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info). If the staging buffer (allocated inside AcquireQuantumInfo from columns/depth) were ever smaller than the 'length' GetQuantumExtent computes for the same columns/depth — e.g. through a colorspace/depth mismatch or a columns value not applied consistently — this is a heap out-of-bounds write (CWE-787); symmetrically ImportQuantumPixels over-reading 'pixels' would be CWE-125. In stock MagickCore both derive from the same image state and match, so no overflow is demonstrated in the shown code; this is the invariant to keep intact.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify in the QuantumInfo implementation that the GetQuantumPixels buffer is allocated with at least GetQuantumExtent(...) bytes for GrayQuantum at depth 1 and the current columns, and that ImportQuantumPixels bounds its read of 'pixels' to that extent.

findings.title: Pad-byte read ignores return value and count
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value / ignored short read (informational)
findings.invariant: The 0/1-byte alignment padding read may fail on a truncated file; correctness relies on a downstream detector rather than an inline check.
findings.failure_mode: When 'length' is odd the code consumes one pad byte but ignores both the returned pointer and 'count'; a truncated trailing pad byte is not flagged here. This is benign for memory safety (the read size is at most 1 and targets the already-sized staging buffer), and the missing byte is caught by the EOFBlob check at line 90 which raises UnexpectedEndOfFile. The reassignment of 'pixels' next iteration before any use means the ignored value is never misused.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm EOFBlob at line 90 is the intended detector for a truncated final pad byte and that ignoring 'count' from this specific read has no other consumer.

audit_checklist: Trace every path after AcquireQuantumInfo (line 60) and confirm DestroyQuantumInfo is called exactly once before each return/throw — especially the line 76 destroy-before-throw and the line 89 tail — with no path that returns while quantum_info is still live and no double free.
Confirm ThrowReaderException and ThrowFileException macro expansions do NOT themselves free quantum_info (so the manual destroys are actually required and not a double free).

Verify GetQuantumExtent(image,quantum_info,GrayQuantum) at line 63 never exceeds the GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info) buffer capacity for the current columns/depth, closing the OOB-write contract on the line 72 ReadBlobStream copy.
Check that ImportQuantumPixels reads at most 'length' bytes of 'pixels' for 'image->columns' GrayQuantum samples.
Confirm SetImageExtent (line 53) enforces engine resource limits so a hostile 65535x65535 declared canvas cannot exhaust memory before decoding.
Validate that the zero-dimension guard (line 46) is the only reachability into pixel decoding and that truncated headers reliably read back as 0.
Confirm EOFBlob (line 90) is the intended detector for a missing trailing alignment/pad byte given the pad-read at lines 81-82 ignores its result.
